text: The patient is positioned in the modified Lloyd Davis position with a right tilt. A central umbilical port is used for the laparoscopic camera. Initial laparoscopy reveals chronic dilatation, purulent fluid in the pelvis, and dilated small bowel. The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks and approaches the inferior mesenteric artery. Monopolar diathermy is used to dissect and expose the artery while preserving surrounding nerves. Hemlock clips are applied to secure the artery before division.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, dividing the inferior mesenteric vein between clips while carefully avoiding injury to the dilated small bowel. The splenic flexure is mobilized by releasing the gastrocolic omentum, with a swab placed over the pancreas visible during the dissection. The left colon is mobilized using monopolar diathermy, focusing on dissection in the clean field and avoiding drainage of the abscess or contaminated colon.

The surgeon focuses on the pelvis, where a sigmoid phlegmon and an abscess between the uterus and sigmoid colon are visible. Inferior dissection is performed to increase the mobility of the mass and protect the ureter and gonadal vessels. The left lateral dissection is challenging due to the phlegmon obstructing the surgical instruments, requiring careful navigation to avoid damaging the left pelvic side wall vessels.

The surgeon opens the abscess cavity using monopolar diathermy, separating the uterus from the diseased sigmoid colon. The abscess is aspirated, and further dissection is performed to assess the possibility of salvaging the uterus. The left pelvic side wall is adherent, requiring careful dissection to avoid injuring the left ureter. The tissues appear hard and fibrotic, necessitating cautious use of energy devices.
